claims.claim_text: The module intro says OPA and Gatekeeper ensure every resource entering the cluster meets security and compliance requirements.
claims.anchor_text: This module covers Open Policy Agent (OPA) and Gatekeeper for policy-as-code admission control, ensuring every resource that enters your cluster meets your security and compliance requirements.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This module covers Open Policy Agent (OPA) and Gatekeeper for policy-as-code admission control, helping you enforce security and compliance requirements on the Kubernetes resources your policies match.
claims.rationale: The original sentence overstates coverage by implying universal enforcement over every resource in the cluster.

claims.claim_text: Gatekeeper can report policy violations in audit/dry-run workflows without blocking existing workloads.
claims.rationale: The official Gatekeeper documentation directly supports this capability, but the host is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Open Policy Agent was created by Styra.
claims.rationale: Styra is the primary-source host for the project-origin claim, but it is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Open Policy Agent was accepted into CNCF in 2018.
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page gives the acceptance date for OPA directly.

claims.claim_text: OPA is used beyond Kubernetes, including Terraform, Envoy, and Kafka.
claims.rationale: OPA's official ecosystem directory documents these integrations, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: OPA is used in CI/CD pipelines.
claims.rationale: OPA's official CI/CD documentation is the right primary source, but the host is not allowlisted.

claims.claim_text: OPA is an open source, general-purpose policy engine that evaluates rules and data to answer policy queries.
claims.span_hint: section: Open Policy Agent (OPA) > What is OPA?
claims.rationale: The upstream README states that OPA is a general-purpose policy engine and explains its query-based evaluation model.

claims.claim_text: The Rego fun-fact claims a specific naming origin in Billund and an auditability advantage over imperative code.
claims.anchor_text: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Rego is named after the Lego brick factory in Billund, Denmark. The creators wanted policies to "snap together" like Lego bricks. The language was designed specifically for policy—it's declarative, making it easier to audit than imperative code.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Rego is OPA's declarative policy language, designed for policy evaluation over structured data.
claims.rationale: The name-origin anecdote is not backed by an allowlisted primary source, so the safest fix is to remove it and keep the core teaching point.

claims.claim_text: The Gatekeeper Library fun-fact claims there are 50+ ready-to-use ConstraintTemplates covering specific policy areas.
claims.anchor_text: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Gatekeeper includes a library of pre-built policies called the [Gatekeeper Library](https://open-policy-agent.github.io/gatekeeper-library/). There are 50+ ready-to-use ConstraintTemplates covering Pod Security Standards, general security, and Kubernetes best practices.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Gatekeeper includes the [Gatekeeper Library](https://open-policy-agent.github.io/gatekeeper-library/), a collection of reusable policies and examples for common Kubernetes security and policy patterns.
claims.rationale: The exact template count is a specific statistic without an allowlisted primary source on hand.

claims.claim_text: The mutation fun-fact claims mutation was one of Gatekeeper's most requested additions and contrasts it with prior use of separate tools like Kyverno or custom webhooks.
claims.anchor_text: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Gatekeeper's mutation feature was one of its most requested additions. Before mutation, teams had to use separate tools like Kyverno or custom webhooks to add default values. Now you can both validate AND mutate with a single tool—enforce that pods run as non-root, and automatically add the setting if developers forget.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Gatekeeper supports mutation alongside validation, so you can enforce settings like `runAsNonRoot` and also assign defaults when matching resources omit them.
claims.rationale: The popularity and historical-comparison claims are unsourced; the rewrite preserves the concrete product capability without uncited history.

claims.claim_text: The war story says a platform team deployed registry policies on Monday and had received 500+ Slack exception requests by Wednesday.
claims.anchor_text: *A platform team deployed strict container registry policies on Monday. By Wednesday, they'd received 500+ Slack messages asking for exceptions.*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *A platform team that deploys strict container registry policies without auditing current image usage can quickly trigger a large wave of exception requests.*
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with fake-precise timing and volume, so the specifics must be removed.

claims.claim_text: The war story says Helm charts pulled from ghcr.io.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Some Helm charts may pull images from registries outside your initial allowlist.
claims.rationale: This is unsourced incident detail attached to the anecdote and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: The war story says logging agents used docker.io/fluent/.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Operational add-ons may depend on images from registries outside your initial allowlist.
claims.rationale: This is unsourced anecdotal detail and should be generalized to the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: The war story says monitoring needed quay.io/prometheus/.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Monitoring components may also depend on registries you did not initially account for.
claims.rationale: This is unsourced anecdotal detail and should be generalized to the operational lesson.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Admission Controllers
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains where admission control fits in the API request path and why it matters for policy enforcement.

further_reading.title: Gatekeeper Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides the upstream overview of Gatekeeper features such as constraints, mutation support, audit, and policy library integration.

further_reading.title: OPA Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides the upstream overview of OPA as a general-purpose policy engine and links to its core integrations and language resources.

notes: Code blocks, tables, diagrams, quiz items, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded per the seed rules. Several product-specific OPA/Gatekeeper claims would be straightforward to source from official docs, but those official hosts are not currently on the allowlist, so they are marked needs_allowlist_expansion where appropriate.
